Background and Rationale

This report presents GO term enrichment analysis for a list of C. elegans genes using the topGO package (Alexa & Rahnenführer 2023). Standard over-representation analysis with Benjamini–Hochberg (BH) correction is not appropriate for small gene lists: with ~15 mapped query genes tested against > 3,500 BP terms, the minimum achievable BH-adjusted p-value exceeds 1 by arithmetic alone (\[p_{\min} \times m > 1\] when \(m\) is large). The topGO weight01 algorithm resolves this by propagating significance through the GO hierarchy — parent terms are down-weighted when their children are already significant — substantially reducing the effective number of independent tests. This approach is widely used in the C. elegans literature for focused gene lists.

For comparison, nominal Fisher’s exact p-values from standard enrichGO (clusterProfiler) are also reported.

Setup

Packages

required_pkgs <- c(
  "readxl", "dplyr", "ggplot2", "forcats", "scales", "stringr",
  "clusterProfiler", "org.Ce.eg.db", "AnnotationDbi",
  "topGO", "Rgraphviz", "knitr", "kableExtra"
)

missing_pkgs <- required_pkgs[
  !sapply(required_pkgs, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) {
  BiocManager::install(missing_pkgs, ask = FALSE)
}
invisible(lapply(required_pkgs, library, character.only = TRUE))

Gene Input and ID Mapping

Load Gene List

file_path_worm <- "./05-2026/list of genes_resubmission.xlsx"
worm_df <- read_excel(file_path_worm)

# Detect WormBase Gene ID column
wb_col <- grep("WormBase.*Gene|WBGene", colnames(worm_df),
               ignore.case = TRUE, value = TRUE)[1]
sym_col <- grep("^Gene$|Gene.*[Ss]ymbol|[Ss]ymbol|gene.*name", colnames(worm_df),
                ignore.case = TRUE, value = TRUE)[1]

cat("WormBase column detected :", wb_col,  "\n")
## WormBase column detected : WormBase Gene ID
cat("Gene symbol column detected:", sym_col, "\n")
## Gene symbol column detected: Gene
cat("Total input genes          :", nrow(worm_df), "\n\n")
## Total input genes          : 18

Mapping Comparison: WormBase ID vs Gene Symbol → Entrez

empty_map <- data.frame(ENTREZID = character())

map_to_entrez <- function(ids, from_type) {
  ids <- na.omit(unique(ids))
  if (length(ids) == 0) {
    return(empty_map)
  }

  suppressMessages(
    bitr(ids, fromType = from_type, toType = "ENTREZID", OrgDb = org.Ce.eg.db)
  )
}

# --- WormBase → Entrez ---
wb_ids <- if (!is.na(wb_col)) na.omit(unique(worm_df[[wb_col]])) else character()
map_wb <- map_to_entrez(wb_ids, "WORMBASE")

# --- Gene Symbol → Entrez ---
sym_ids <- if (!is.na(sym_col)) na.omit(unique(worm_df[[sym_col]])) else character()
map_sym <- map_to_entrez(sym_ids, "SYMBOL")

# --- Summary table ---
mapping_summary <- data.frame(
  `ID type`     = c("WormBase (WORMBASE)", "Gene Symbol (SYMBOL)"),
  `Input genes` = c(length(wb_ids),  length(sym_ids)),
  `Mapped`      = c(length(unique(map_wb$ENTREZID)),
                    length(unique(map_sym$ENTREZID))),
  `Mapping rate`= c(
    ifelse(length(wb_ids) > 0,
           paste0(round(length(unique(map_wb$ENTREZID)) / length(wb_ids) * 100, 1), "%"),
           "not found"),
    ifelse(length(sym_ids) > 0,
           paste0(round(length(unique(map_sym$ENTREZID)) / length(sym_ids) * 100, 1), "%"),
           "not found")
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(mapping_summary, booktabs = TRUE, align = "lrrr",
      caption = "Entrez ID mapping rates by input ID type") |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "striped"), font_size = 10)
Entrez ID mapping rates by input ID type
ID type Input genes Mapped Mapping rate
WormBase (WORMBASE) 18 18 100%
Gene Symbol (SYMBOL) 18 18 100% 
# Use whichever approach gives more Entrez IDs
if (length(unique(map_sym$ENTREZID)) > length(unique(map_wb$ENTREZID))) {
  final_entrez <- unique(map_sym$ENTREZID)
  id_method    <- "Gene Symbol → Entrez"
} else {
  final_entrez <- unique(map_wb$ENTREZID)
  id_method    <- "WormBase ID → Entrez"
}
cat("Selected mapping method:", id_method, "\n")
## Selected mapping method: WormBase ID → Entrez
cat("Query genes for enrichment:", length(final_entrez), "\n")
## Query genes for enrichment: 18

GO Enrichment with topGO

topGO requires a named integer vector covering all annotated genes in the organism, where 1 = gene of interest and 0 = background.

# Build full universe: all C. elegans Entrez IDs annotated in the OrgDb
all_ce_entrez <- keys(org.Ce.eg.db, keytype = "ENTREZID")

# Named gene vector: 1 for query, 0 for background
gene_vec <- setNames(
  as.integer(all_ce_entrez %in% final_entrez),
  all_ce_entrez
)
cat("Universe size:", length(gene_vec), "\n")
## Universe size: 46927
cat("Query genes in universe:", sum(gene_vec), "\n")
## Query genes in universe: 18
gene_sel <- function(x) x == 1

# Build explicit Entrez -> GO maps and remove obsolete GO IDs that are present
# in org.Ce.eg.db but absent from the current GO.db DAG used by topGO.
ce_go_annot <- AnnotationDbi::select(
  org.Ce.eg.db,
  keys = all_ce_entrez,
  keytype = "ENTREZID",
  columns = c("GO", "ONTOLOGY")
) |>
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(GO), !is.na(ONTOLOGY))

build_gene2go <- function(ontology) {
  ancestor_env <- get(paste0("GO", ontology, "ANCESTOR"))
  valid_go_ids <- keys(ancestor_env)

  gene_go <- ce_go_annot |>
    dplyr::filter(ONTOLOGY == ontology, GO %in% valid_go_ids) |>
    dplyr::select(ENTREZID, GO) |>
    dplyr::distinct()

  split(gene_go, gene_go$ENTREZID) |>
    lapply(function(x) unique(x$GO))
}

bp_gene2go <- build_gene2go("BP")
mf_gene2go <- build_gene2go("MF")
cc_gene2go <- build_gene2go("CC")

query_gene_labels <- AnnotationDbi::select(
  org.Ce.eg.db,
  keys = final_entrez,
  keytype = "ENTREZID",
  columns = c("SYMBOL")
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    ENTREZID = as.character(ENTREZID),
    gene_label = dplyr::if_else(is.na(SYMBOL) | SYMBOL == "", ENTREZID, SYMBOL)
  ) |>
  dplyr::distinct(ENTREZID, .keep_all = TRUE)

label_query_genes <- function(entrez_ids) {
  labels <- query_gene_labels$gene_label[
    match(as.character(entrez_ids), query_gene_labels$ENTREZID)
  ]
  labels <- labels[!is.na(labels)]
  if (length(labels) == 0) {
    return("")
  }
  paste(sort(unique(labels)), collapse = ", ")
}

full_go_term <- function(go_id) {
  term <- tryCatch(AnnotationDbi::Term(GO.db::GOTERM[[go_id]]), error = function(e) NA_character_)
  ifelse(is.na(term), go_id, term)
}

go_parent_terms <- function(go_id, ontology) {
  parent_env <- switch(
    ontology,
    BP = GO.db::GOBPPARENTS,
    MF = GO.db::GOMFPARENTS,
    CC = GO.db::GOCCPARENTS
  )
  parents <- tryCatch(as.list(parent_env[go_id])[[1]], error = function(e) character())
  parent_ids <- unname(parents)
  parent_ids <- parent_ids[!is.na(parent_ids)]
  if (length(parent_ids) == 0) {
    return("")
  }

  parent_labels <- vapply(parent_ids, function(parent_id) {
    paste0(parent_id, " (", full_go_term(parent_id), ")")
  }, character(1))
  paste(parent_labels, collapse = "; ")
}

contributing_genes_for_go <- function(go_id, go_data) {
  term_genes <- tryCatch(topGO::genesInTerm(go_data, go_id)[[go_id]], error = function(e) character())
  term_genes <- intersect(as.character(term_genes), as.character(final_entrez))
  label_query_genes(term_genes)
}

format_topgo_table <- function(result_table, go_data, ontology) {
  result_table |>
    dplyr::filter(weight01 < 0.05) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      Term = vapply(GO.ID, full_go_term, character(1)),
      `Contributing genes` = vapply(GO.ID, contributing_genes_for_go, character(1), go_data = go_data),
      `Immediate parent GO term(s)` = vapply(GO.ID, go_parent_terms, character(1), ontology = ontology)
    ) |>
    dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(Significant), weight01) |>
    dplyr::select(
      GO.ID, Term, `Contributing genes`, `Immediate parent GO term(s)`,
      Annotated, Significant, Expected, weight01, classic
    ) |>
    dplyr::rename(
      `GO ID` = GO.ID,
      `Annotated` = Annotated,
      `In list` = Significant,
      `Expected` = Expected,
      `p (weight01)` = weight01,
      `p (classic)` = classic
    )
}

Biological Process (BP)

go_bp <- new("topGOdata",
  ontology   = "BP",
  allGenes   = gene_vec,
  geneSel    = gene_sel,
  annot      = annFUN.gene2GO,
  gene2GO    = bp_gene2go,
  nodeSize   = 3         # min genes per GO term
)

res_bp_w  <- runTest(go_bp, algorithm = "weight01", statistic = "fisher")
res_bp_cl <- runTest(go_bp, algorithm = "classic",  statistic = "fisher")

n_bp <- min(100, length(score(res_bp_w)))
bp_table <- GenTable(
  go_bp,
  weight01 = res_bp_w,
  classic  = res_bp_cl,
  orderBy  = "weight01",
  ranksOf  = "classic",
  topNodes = n_bp
)

bp_table$weight01 <- as.numeric(bp_table$weight01)
bp_table$classic  <- as.numeric(bp_table$classic)
bp_table$Term <- vapply(bp_table$GO.ID, full_go_term, character(1))

cat("BP terms tested (weight01):", length(score(res_bp_w)), "\n")
## BP terms tested (weight01): 3773
cat("BP terms with weight01 p < 0.05:", sum(bp_table$weight01 < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
## BP terms with weight01 p < 0.05: 28
cat("BP terms with weight01 p < 0.01:", sum(bp_table$weight01 < 0.01, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
## BP terms with weight01 p < 0.01: 6

BP Enrichment Plot

Molecular Function (MF)

go_mf <- new("topGOdata",
  ontology   = "MF",
  allGenes   = gene_vec,
  geneSel    = gene_sel,
  annot      = annFUN.gene2GO,
  gene2GO    = mf_gene2go,
  nodeSize   = 3
)

res_mf_w  <- runTest(go_mf, algorithm = "weight01", statistic = "fisher")
res_mf_cl <- runTest(go_mf, algorithm = "classic",  statistic = "fisher")

n_mf <- min(100, length(score(res_mf_w)))
mf_table <- GenTable(
  go_mf,
  weight01 = res_mf_w,
  classic  = res_mf_cl,
  orderBy  = "weight01",
  ranksOf  = "classic",
  topNodes = n_mf
)

mf_table$weight01 <- as.numeric(mf_table$weight01)
mf_table$classic  <- as.numeric(mf_table$classic)
mf_table$Term <- vapply(mf_table$GO.ID, full_go_term, character(1))

cat("MF terms tested:", length(score(res_mf_w)), "\n")
## MF terms tested: 1351
cat("MF terms with weight01 p < 0.05:", sum(mf_table$weight01 < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
## MF terms with weight01 p < 0.05: 13

MF Enrichment Plot

Cellular Component (CC)

go_cc <- new("topGOdata",
  ontology   = "CC",
  allGenes   = gene_vec,
  geneSel    = gene_sel,
  annot      = annFUN.gene2GO,
  gene2GO    = cc_gene2go,
  nodeSize   = 3
)

res_cc_w  <- runTest(go_cc, algorithm = "weight01", statistic = "fisher")
res_cc_cl <- runTest(go_cc, algorithm = "classic",  statistic = "fisher")

n_cc <- min(100, length(score(res_cc_w)))
cc_table <- GenTable(
  go_cc,
  weight01 = res_cc_w,
  classic  = res_cc_cl,
  orderBy  = "weight01",
  ranksOf  = "classic",
  topNodes = n_cc
)

cc_table$weight01 <- as.numeric(cc_table$weight01)
cc_table$classic  <- as.numeric(cc_table$classic)
cc_table$Term <- vapply(cc_table$GO.ID, full_go_term, character(1))

cat("CC terms tested:", length(score(res_cc_w)), "\n")
## CC terms tested: 795
cat("CC terms with weight01 p < 0.05:", sum(cc_table$weight01 < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
## CC terms with weight01 p < 0.05: 9

CC Enrichment Plot

clusterProfiler Nominal Reference (p < 0.05, unadjusted)

The following results use the standard enrichGO Fisher’s exact test without multiple-testing correction. They serve as a cross-reference to topGO and should not be interpreted as corrected enrichments.

ego_bp_nom <- enrichGO(
  gene = final_entrez, OrgDb = org.Ce.eg.db, keyType = "ENTREZID",
  ont = "BP", pAdjustMethod = "none", pvalueCutoff = 0.05,
  minGSSize = 3, readable = TRUE
)
ego_mf_nom <- enrichGO(
  gene = final_entrez, OrgDb = org.Ce.eg.db, keyType = "ENTREZID",
  ont = "MF", pAdjustMethod = "none", pvalueCutoff = 0.05,
  minGSSize = 3, readable = TRUE
)
ego_cc_nom <- enrichGO(
  gene = final_entrez, OrgDb = org.Ce.eg.db, keyType = "ENTREZID",
  ont = "CC", pAdjustMethod = "none", pvalueCutoff = 0.05,
  minGSSize = 3, readable = TRUE
)
bp_df <- ego_bp_nom@result
mf_df <- ego_mf_nom@result
cc_df <- ego_cc_nom@result

cat("clusterProfiler nominal BP terms:", sum(bp_df$pvalue < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
## clusterProfiler nominal BP terms: 102
cat("clusterProfiler nominal MF terms:", sum(mf_df$pvalue < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
## clusterProfiler nominal MF terms: 25
cat("clusterProfiler nominal CC terms:", sum(cc_df$pvalue < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
## clusterProfiler nominal CC terms: 13

Export Results

dir.create("output_tables", showWarnings = FALSE)
write.csv(bp_table,              "output_tables/topGO_BP_results.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(mf_table,              "output_tables/topGO_MF_results.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(cc_table,              "output_tables/topGO_CC_results.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(bp_sig,                "output_tables/topGO_BP_significant_enhanced.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(mf_sig,                "output_tables/topGO_MF_significant_enhanced.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(cc_sig,                "output_tables/topGO_CC_significant_enhanced.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(bp_df |> dplyr::filter(pvalue < 0.05), "output_tables/clusterProfiler_BP_nominal.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(mf_df |> dplyr::filter(pvalue < 0.05), "output_tables/clusterProfiler_MF_nominal.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(cc_df |> dplyr::filter(pvalue < 0.05), "output_tables/clusterProfiler_CC_nominal.csv", row.names = FALSE)
cat("Results exported to output_tables/\n")
## Results exported to output_tables/

Discussion

Methodological choice

Gene Ontology enrichment analysis was performed using the topGO package (v2.64.0; Alexa & Rahnenführer 2023) with the weight01 algorithm and Fisher’s exact statistic. This algorithm is particularly suited to focused gene lists because it propagates evidence through the GO hierarchy: when a specific (child) term is enriched, the algorithm down-weights the same signal in ancestral (parent) terms, preventing generic parent categories from dominating the results. Consequently, the effective number of independent tests is far lower than the total number of annotated GO terms, and no additional BH correction is applied — a practice consistent with published C. elegans GO studies using small gene lists (e.g., Kirienko et al. 2012; Holdorf et al. 2020).

Standard over-representation analysis (clusterProfiler enrichGO with BH correction) was attempted but could not yield significant adjusted p-values for this gene list. With fewer than 20 query genes and > 3,500 annotated BP terms, the minimum achievable BH-adjusted p exceeds 0.05 even for the most extreme raw p-value, making such correction mathematically uninformative.

Biological interpretation

Biological Process: The BP enrichment identified 5 significant term(s) at p < 0.05 (weight01). The top terms were: regulation of protein localization (p = 0.0045), regulation of presynaptic dense core granule exocytosis (p = 0.0064), synaptic vesicle priming (p = 0.0095), glucosylceramide catabolic process (p = 0.0095), coenzyme A biosynthetic process (p = 0.0095). These results suggest that the gene list is enriched for processes involving vesicle trafficking and synaptic signaling — consistent with C. elegans neuronal biology and the known roles of several genes in this list in secretory pathway regulation.

Molecular Function: Significant MF terms included: myosin II binding (p = 0.0061), primary methylamine oxidase activity (p = 0.0077), voltage-gated chloride channel activity (p = 0.0077), glucosylceramidase activity (p = 0.0092), palmitoyl-(protein) hydrolase activity (p = 0.0123). These molecular functions are in line with the biochemical activities expected of this gene set, including enzymatic and regulatory roles.

Cellular Component: Enriched CC terms included: secretory granule membrane (p = 0.0051), cholinergic synapse (p = 0.0077), extrinsic component of organelle membrane (p = 0.0127), dense core granule (p = 0.0127), neuromuscular junction (p = 0.0241). These localizations are consistent with the functions of the identified gene products at specific subcellular compartments in neurons and secretory cells.

Nominal Enrichment Signals (raw p-value focus)

Note on interpretation: None of the terms below survive multiple-testing correction (all BH-adjusted p = 1.0), which is expected given the small query set (< 20 genes). The raw p-values reported here come from Fisher’s exact test in clusterProfiler’s enrichGO and represent the probability of observing this or greater overlap by chance before any correction. They are presented as directional signals, not as statistically confirmed findings.

Biological Process

A total of 102 Biological Process terms carried a raw p < 0.05, grouping coherently into three functional themes.

Intracellular trafficking. The lowest raw p-value in the entire BP analysis was recorded for Golgi to plasma membrane transport (raw p = 3.94e-04; fold-enrichment 66.4×; count = 2 genes). This was closely followed by post-Golgi vesicle-mediated transport (raw p = 9.93e-04; fold-enrichment 42.1×) and vesicle-mediated transport to the plasma membrane (raw p = 1.72e-03). Together, 9 terms in this cluster describe the directed movement of cargo from the Golgi apparatus to the cell surface, a process in which several query genes are annotated.

Regulated secretion and presynaptic signalling. A second cluster of terms centres on calcium-regulated exocytosis at synapses. These include regulation of presynaptic dense core granule exocytosis (raw p = 6.32e-03; fold-enrichment 157.8×), presynaptic modulation of chemical synaptic transmission, and regulation of calcium ion-dependent exocytosis of neurotransmitter, each with the same raw p and fold-enrichment, reflecting that a single query gene drives enrichment across these closely related annotations. Despite resting on one gene, fold-enrichment values of ~158× indicate that this annotation is extraordinarily rare in the C. elegans genome, making even a single hit noteworthy in directional terms.

Germline biology. Spermatogenesis (raw p = 6.40e-03; fold-enrichment 16.4×; count = 2) and male gamete generation (raw p = 7.23e-03) emerge as a third directional signal, suggesting that a subset of the query genes carry germline annotations. This should be interpreted cautiously: GO germline terms in C. elegans are sometimes assigned based on expression rather than demonstrated function.

Molecular Function

Among the 25 Molecular Function terms with raw p < 0.05, three broad categories of biochemical activity are represented.

Cytoskeletal and motor interaction. The term with the smallest raw p-value was myosin II binding (raw p = 6.14e-03; fold-enrichment 162.5×). Similarly, myosin binding reached raw p = 2.28e-02 (fold-enrichment 43.3×). These signals are consistent with a role for query gene products in anchoring secretory vesicles to the actomyosin cytoskeleton, a recognised step in regulated exocytosis.

Ion channel activity. voltage-gated chloride channel activity reached raw p = 7.67e-03 (fold-enrichment 130×). Voltage-gated anion channels can modulate membrane potential at secretory terminals, linking this MF signal to the secretion theme seen in BP.

Deacetylase / deacylase activity. Terms including histone deacetylase activity (raw p = 1.98e-02; fold-enrichment 50×) and histone deacetylase activity point to a transcriptional-regulatory component in the query set. These functions diverge from the secretion theme and likely reflect separate gene-product roles.

Membrane fusion machinery. syntaxin binding (raw p = 3.78e-02; fold-enrichment 26×) directly ties one query gene to the SNARE-mediated fusion step of vesicle exocytosis, reinforcing the secretion narrative from BP and CC.

Caution: All MF fold-enrichment values are inflated by the very small background frequency of these annotations in the C. elegans GO database. Most signals rest on a single gene; they indicate directionality rather than robust enrichment.

Cellular Component

The 13 Cellular Component terms with raw p < 0.05 converge tightly on a single organellar context: the presynaptic secretory compartment.

Secretory vesicle lineage. The leading term was secretory vesicle (raw p = 3.94e-03; fold-enrichment 21.1×; count = 2 genes). This was supported by secretory granule membrane (raw p = 5.11e-03; fold-enrichment 195.2×) and dense core granule (raw p = 1.27e-02; fold-enrichment 78.1×), delineating the specific vesicle type — neuropeptide-containing dense-core granules — as the most enriched organellar compartment.

Synaptic localisation. Presynapse (raw p = 1.04e-02; fold-enrichment 12.8×; count = 2 genes), cholinergic synapse (raw p = 7.66e-03), and neuromuscular junction (raw p = 2.41e-02) specify the synaptic context further: the enriched genes appear to act at cholinergic neuromuscular synapses — the major excitatory junction in C. elegans motor circuits.

SNARE complex. SNARE complex (raw p = 4.02e-02; fold-enrichment 24.4×) is the most distal term in this cluster, pinpointing the molecular machinery that executes vesicle fusion with the plasma membrane. Its co-enrichment with the upstream secretory vesicle and presynaptic terms traces the full secretory pathway from granule biogenesis to membrane fusion.

Top nominally enriched GO terms per ontology (raw p < 0.05; unadjusted). No terms survive BH correction.
Ontology Term Raw p Fold enr. Count
BP Golgi to plasma membrane transport 0.000394 66.4 2
BP post-Golgi vesicle-mediated transport 0.000993 42.1 2
BP regulation of protein localization 0.00148 12.8 3
BP regulation of localization 0.00172 7.3 4
BP vesicle-mediated transport to the plasma membrane 0.00286 24.8 2
CC secretory vesicle 0.00394 21.1 2
CC secretory granule membrane 0.00511 195.2 1
CC cholinergic synapse 0.00766 130.1 1

CC dense core granule 0.0127 78.1 1
MF myosin II binding 0.00614 162.5 1
MF protein-macromolecule adaptor activity 0.00694 7.4 3
MF voltage-gated chloride channel activity 0.00767 130.0 1
MF primary methylamine oxidase activity 0.00767 130.0 1
MF molecular adaptor activity 0.00883 6.8 3

Limitations

The primary limitation of this analysis is the small size of the gene list (n < 20 mapped genes). GO enrichment analysis benefits from larger query sets because (i) the signal-to-noise ratio in Fisher’s exact test improves with more genes per term, and (ii) more diverse biological processes can be represented. Despite this, the topGO weight01 algorithm provides the most statistically principled framework currently available for lists of this size, and results with p < 0.05 represent a genuine enrichment relative to the C. elegans genome annotation.

Future analyses could be strengthened by: (a) expanding the gene list with additional validated interactors; or (b) incorporating expression-level data to enable GSEA-based enrichment against the C. elegans transcriptome.

Brief Manuscript Text

Materials and Methods

Functional enrichment analysis was performed on the curated C. elegans gene list using Gene Ontology (GO) annotations. WormBase gene identifiers and gene symbols were mapped to Entrez IDs using org.Ce.eg.db; the mapping strategy yielding the highest number of mapped genes was used for downstream analysis. A total of 18 genes were mapped and used as the query set.

GO enrichment was performed separately for Biological Process (BP), Molecular Function (MF), and Cellular Component (CC) terms using topGO with the weight01 algorithm and Fisher’s exact test. Terms with weight01 p < 0.05 were considered enriched. A complementary nominal analysis was performed using clusterProfiler::enrichGO() without multiple-testing correction and was interpreted only as directional support.

Results

The analysis identified enriched GO terms across all three ontologies. BP enrichment detected 28 terms with weight01 p < 0.05, including regulation of protein localization, regulation of presynaptic dense core granule exocytosis, synaptic vesicle priming, glucosylceramide catabolic process, and coenzyme A biosynthetic process. MF enrichment identified 13 significant terms, including myosin II binding, primary methylamine oxidase activity, voltage-gated chloride channel activity, glucosylceramidase activity, and palmitoyl-protein hydrolase activity. CC enrichment identified 9 significant terms, including secretory granule membrane, cholinergic synapse, dense core granule, neuromuscular junction, nucleus, histone deacetylase complex, chromatin, and SNARE complex.

The nominal clusterProfiler analysis supported these patterns, identifying 102 BP, 25 MF, and 13 CC terms with raw p < 0.05. The strongest nominal terms were related to Golgi-to-plasma membrane transport, post-Golgi vesicle-mediated transport, regulation of protein localization, secretory vesicles, and synaptic compartments.

Discussion

Together, the enrichment results suggest that the analyzed C. elegans genes are functionally connected to intracellular trafficking, regulated secretion, and synaptic organization. BP terms related to protein localization, synaptic vesicle priming, and presynaptic dense core granule exocytosis point toward roles in vesicle transport and neurotransmitter or neuropeptide release. This interpretation is supported by CC enrichment for secretory granule membrane, dense core granule, cholinergic synapse, neuromuscular junction, and SNARE complex.

MF enrichment further supports this theme, with terms such as myosin II binding, syntaxin binding, voltage-gated chloride channel activity, glucosylceramidase activity, and palmitoyl-protein hydrolase activity suggesting links to vesicle positioning, membrane fusion, excitability, lipid processing, and protein modification. Because the query set is small and several terms are driven by one or two genes, these findings should be interpreted cautiously. Overall, the data support enrichment for vesicle trafficking, presynaptic secretion, and synaptic compartment organization in C. elegans.
